Startups are defined as independent, technology-enabled organizations under five years old, focused on scalable, high-growth business models. Unlike small businesses, they aim to disrupt industries, with examples like Netflix and Tesla. The startup lifecycle includes stages from pre-seed to exit, such as IPOs or acquisitions. Investors can participate through direct angel investing, equity crowdfunding (which saw $343.6 million in 2024), venture capital funds, or secondary markets. While 90% of startups fail, successful ones can yield significant returns, though investors face risks like illiquidity and information asymmetry.
